title: iOS Project Structure вЂ“ Organizing Large-Scale Swift Apps

meta.title: iOS Project Structure Guide: Clean Architecture & Xcode Groups (2026)
meta.description: Learn how to organize iOS projects for scalability: Clean Architecture layers (Presentation, Domain, Data), module separation, feature-based grouping, and Xcode folder conventions for teams.

content.value: Why Project Structure Matters

A poorly organized iOS project leads to merge conflicts, slow compilation, and developer frustration. A good structure is **feature-based**, **layered**, and **testable**. This guide presents a battle-tested structure for SwiftUI + UIKit apps using Clean Architecture principles, suitable for teams of 2вЂ“20 developers.
1. High-Level Folder Layout
At the root level, separate your code into these main groups. This works for both SwiftUI and UIKit projects.

MyApp/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ App/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Core/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Features/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Shared/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Services/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Resources/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Networking/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Persistence/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Utilities/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Extensions/
в””в”Ђв”Ђ Tests/
2. Detailed Breakdown by Layer
Each top-level folder has a specific responsibility. Below is the complete tree with explanations.

MyApp/
в”‚
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ App/                                 # App lifecycle & entry point
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ MyAppApp.swift                   # @main for SwiftUI
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ AppDelegate.swift                # UIKit lifecycle (if needed)
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ SceneDelegate.swift
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ Info.plist
в”‚
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Core/                                # Core architecture & base classes
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Architecture/
в”‚   в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ BaseView.swift
в”‚   в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ BaseViewModel.swift
в”‚   в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ BaseViewController.swift    # UIKit
в”‚   в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ CoordinatorProtocol.swift
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Routing/
в”‚   в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ AppRouter.swift
в”‚   в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Route.swift
в”‚   в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ DeepLinkHandler.swift

в”‚       в”њв”Ђв”Ђ AppContainer.swift
в”‚       в”њв”Ђв”Ђ ServiceLocator.swift
в”‚       в””в”Ђв”Ђ Assemblies/

в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Features/                            # Feature modules (grouped by domain)

в”‚   в”‚
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Dashboard/

в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ Profile/

в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Shared/                              # Cross-feature UI & logic

в”‚   в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Validators/
в”‚   в”‚   в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ EmailValidator.swift
в”‚   в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Formatters/
в”‚   в”‚   в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ DateFormatter+Custom.swift
в”‚   в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ Helpers/
в”‚   в”‚       в””в”Ђв”Ђ KeychainWrapper.swift

в”‚       в”њв”Ђв”Ђ String+Extensions.swift

в”‚
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Services/                            # Global services
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Analytics/

в”‚   в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ AnalyticsEvent.swift

в”‚   в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ PushNotificationService.swift

в”‚   в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ LoggerService.swift

в”‚       в””в”Ђв”Ђ FeatureFlagService.swift
в”‚
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Networking/                          # API layer
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ APIClient.swift
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Endpoints.swift
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ HTTPMethod.swift
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Middleware/
в”‚   в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ AuthInterceptor.swift

в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ Mock/
в”‚       в””в”Ђв”Ђ MockAPIClient.swift

    в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ Features/

        в”њв”Ђв”Ђ MockRepositories/
        в””в”Ђв”Ђ MockServices/

# Xcode Project Groups (same hierarchy but note:)
# - Yellow folders = Group (logical, no physical folder)
# - Blue folders = Folder Reference (physical folder, preserves structure)
3. Clean Architecture in Practice

Inside each `Feature/` module, we follow Clean Architecture with three layers:

- **Presentation** (UI + ViewModels) вЂ“ depends only on Domain.
- **Domain** (Business logic + Entities) вЂ“ NO dependencies on UIKit, SwiftUI, or networking. Pure Swift.
- **Data** (Repositories + DataSources) вЂ“ implements Domain protocols.

**Direction of dependencies:** `Presentation в†’ Domain в†ђ Data` (Dependency Inversion via protocols).
4. Module vs Feature vs Layer

For **large apps** (10+ screens, 5+ developers), consider breaking into **Swift packages** or **Xcode projects**:

| Level | When to use | Example |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| **Feature Module** | Screens that work together | Auth, Checkout, Settings |
| **Shared Module** | Code reused across features | UI Components, Networking, Core |
| **App Module** | Glues everything together | Main app target |

**Modular structure example:**
```
MyApp.xcworkspace/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ App/ (main app target)
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Modules/
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ CorePackage/ (Swift package)
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ NetworkingPackage/
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ UIPackage/
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ FeatureAuth/
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ FeatureDashboard/
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ FeatureProfile/
в””в”Ђв”Ђ Tests/
```
5. Xcode Groups vs Folder References

| Feature | Group (Yellow) | Folder Reference (Blue) |
|---------|---------------|------------------------|
| Physical folder | Optional | Required |
| File organization | Logical only | Mirrors disk |
| Nested resources | вќЊ Won't compile | вњ… Works for resources |
| When to use | Swift code files | Assets, JSON, HTML, Bundled files |

**Best practice:** Use Groups for `.swift` files. Use Folder References for resource folders (fonts, JSON, HTML, videos).
6. Naming Conventions

Consistent naming prevents confusion:

- **View:** `LoginView.swift`, `ProfileHeaderView.swift`
- **ViewModel:** `LoginViewModel.swift` (SwiftUI), `LoginViewController.swift` + `LoginViewModel.swift` (UIKit + MVVM)
- **UseCase:** `LoginUseCase.swift`, `FetchProductsUseCase.swift`
- **Repository Protocol:** `AuthRepositoryProtocol.swift`
- **Repository Impl:** `AuthRepositoryImpl.swift`
- **DTO:** `LoginRequestDTO.swift`, `UserResponseDTO.swift`
- **Service:** `AnalyticsService.swift`, `PushNotificationService.swift`
- **Coordinator:** `AuthCoordinator.swift`
- **Factory:** `ViewModelFactory.swift`
7. Project Configuration Files

Keep configuration at the root of your repo (not inside Xcode project):

```
MyApp/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ .swiftlint.yml              # Linting rules
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ .gitignore
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ fastlane/
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ Fastfile                # CI/CD automation
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ project.yml                 # XcodeGen (optional but recommended)
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Tuist/
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ ProjectDescriptionHelpers/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Makefile                    # Common tasks (test, lint, format)
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в””в”Ђв”Ђ config/
    в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Debug.xcconfig
    в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Release.xcconfig
    в””в”Ђв”Ђ Shared.xcconfig
```
8. Common Anti-Patterns to Avoid

вќЊ **Massive View Controller** вЂ“ Move logic to ViewModels or UseCases.

вќЊ **God Helpers** вЂ“ `SharedUtils.swift` with 5000 lines. Break into small, focused files.

вќЊ **Circular Dependencies** вЂ“ Feature A imports Feature B, and B imports A. Use shared Core module.

вќЊ **Deep Nesting** вЂ“ `Features/Auth/Presentation/Views/Subviews/Components/Button.swift` вЂ“ Keep max 4 levels.

вќЊ **Ignoring Tests** вЂ“ Mirror your main structure under `Tests/` from day one.

вќЊ **Mixing UI Frameworks** вЂ“ Avoid UIKit + SwiftUI in same feature without clear boundary.

faq.question: Should I use groups or folder references for Swift files?
faq.answer: Always use **groups** (yellow folders) for Swift code. Xcode automatically maps them to the filesystem, but you can organize logically without mirroring disk structure. Use **folder references** (blue) only for resource bundles (fonts, HTML, JSON) that need to preserve their folder hierarchy at runtime.

faq.question: How do I decide when to create a new feature module?
faq.answer:
Create a new feature folder when:
- The screen has its own distinct responsibility (e.g., Login vs Dashboard).
- It has at least 3-5 files (View, ViewModel, UseCase, Repository).
- It can be developed independently by a different team member.
- It has minimal coupling to other features (except through Core).

faq.question: What's the difference between Shared, Core, and Utilities?
faq.answer:
- **Core**: Base classes, protocols, DI containers вЂ“ defines your architecture.
- **Shared**: UI components, themes, cross-feature logic вЂ“ used by multiple features.
- **Utilities**: Pure functions with no side effects (crypto, math, formatters) вЂ“ no dependencies on the app.

faq.question: How do I handle dependency injection with this structure?
faq.answer: Use a container per feature. Create `AppContainer.swift` in Core/DI that registers all global services. Each feature can have its own assembler. In SwiftUI, pass dependencies via `@EnvironmentObject` or custom initializers. For UIKit, use constructor injection in ViewControllers. Avoid Service Locator anti-pattern.

faq.question: Should I use Swift Packages for modularization?
faq.answers: Yes, for teams of 5+ or apps with 20+ features. Benefits: explicit dependencies, faster compile times (Xcode builds only changed packages), and reusability across projects. Start with in-repo packages (not external) to avoid overhead.

quiz.question: Which layer in Clean Architecture should have NO dependencies on UIKit, SwiftUI, or networking frameworks?
What color are Xcode groups (logical folders) in the project navigator?
In a feature module following Clean Architecture, where should the repository protocol be defined?
Which of these is an anti-pattern in iOS project structure?
options: Using a Shared folder for reusable components
Organizing by feature instead of by type
